coredata.dc:title: Rice straw derived cellulose nanofibers modified with L-histidine for ultra-trace fluorometric assay of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) in aqueous medium
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Vijay
Rattan, Gaurav
Tewatia, Preeti
Kaur, Manpreet
Pathania, Deepak
Singhal, Sonal
Kaushik, Anupama
coredata.dc:description: Through this study, an effort has been made to eliminate the problems of rice straw burning and pollution due to heavy metals by utilizing cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) derived from rice straw to develop effective fluorescent sensors for detection of chromium Cr(VI) and mercury Hg(II), concurrently. CNFs were modified using L-histidine (CHIS) to yield cellulosic fluorophore, not reported hitherto. Characterization using X-ray Powder Diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), indicated partial impairment of crystalline structure of cellulose with conversion to CHIS, however the sensor was thermally more stable as 40% mass residue remained even after 700 °C. Fluorescence response exhibited substantial quenching with Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions, with detection limits of 2.236 nM and 3.97 nM, respectively. The XPS studies established strong interactions of CHIS with Hg(II) ions via imine groups and Cr(VI) ions with imine as well as amine group on CHIS resulting in substantial quenching. Further, when excited at different wavelength under fluorescence microscope, CHIS indicated suppression of fluorescence effect with adsorption of heavy metal ions. The sensor was highly selective, stable with time and pH with excellent usability up to 4 cycles. Thus, CHIS present a facile and robust platform for simultaneous detection and real time monitoring of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) metal ions.

head.ce:title: Rice straw derived cellulose nanofibers modified with L-histidine for ultra-trace fluorometric assay of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) in aqueous medium

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Through this study, an effort has been made to eliminate the problems of rice straw burning and pollution due to heavy metals by utilizing cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) derived from rice straw to develop effective fluorescent sensors for detection of chromium Cr(VI) and mercury Hg(II), concurrently. CNFs were modified using L-histidine (CHIS) to yield cellulosic fluorophore, not reported hitherto. Characterization using X-ray Powder Diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), indicated partial impairment of crystalline structure of cellulose with conversion to CHIS, however the sensor was thermally more stable as 40% mass residue remained even after 700 °C. Fluorescence response exhibited substantial quenching with Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions, with detection limits of 2.236 nM and 3.97 nM, respectively. The XPS studies established strong interactions of CHIS with Hg(II) ions via imine groups and Cr(VI) ions with imine as well as amine group on CHIS resulting in substantial quenching. Further, when excited at different wavelength under fluorescence microscope, CHIS indicated suppression of fluorescence effect with adsorption of heavy metal ions. The sensor was highly selective, stable with time and pH with excellent usability up to 4 cycles. Thus, CHIS present a facile and robust platform for simultaneous detection and real time monitoring of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For last few decades, contamination of water bodies by heavy metal ions due to rapid urbanization and rising industrialization has become a grave global concern. (Chai et al., 2021; Guo et al., 2021; Jadhav et al., 2020; Jing et al., 2022; Kumar et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021; Salem et al., 2020). Accumulation of these heavy metals in ecological systems raise toxicity in environment and endangers human health. Among heavy metal ions, Cr(VI) and Hg(II) are highly toxic even at a very low concentrations as they lack any physiological function (Krzywy et al., 2011). They are capable of displacing essential microelements in living beings from metalloenzymes (Monastero et al., 2018). Chromium exists majorly in two stable oxidation states, Cr(III) and Cr(VI), wherein chromium ions in Cr(VI) oxidation state is 100 times more lethal to human health and environment than species in Cr(III) oxidation state and has been identified as Group A contaminant by Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) with maximum allowable concentration in drinking water as 0.05 mg L−1 (Sinha et al., 2018). In contrast to Cr(VI), Hg(II) exposure in human beings can lead to skin, respiratory and gastrointestinal irritation and in acute cases may cause failure of kidney, neuron, eyes and immune system apart from cognitive disorders (Balasurya et al., 2020; Chai et al., 2021; Danish et al., 2020; Nandanwar et al., 2022; Rajput et al., 2022; Xia et al., 2020). Due to these deadly effects, World health organization has considered Hg(II) as one of the top ten chemicals of major health concern (World Health Organisation, 2017). It is thus imperative to design and develop precise analytical tools for detection of these metal ions in trace amount in water sources.

A large variety of methods have been reported for analysis and detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions including flame atomic emission spectroscopy (Ghaedi et al., 2006), surface enhanced Raman scattering, inductively couples plasma mass spectroscopy (Allibone et al., 1999; Wu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022b), ion selective electrode and calorimetry (Monisha et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2022a). However, most of these techniques are cumbersome, non-portable, require expensive and sophisticated instrumentation and extensive sample preparation. On the contrary, fluorescence-based chemo-sensors are simple, highly sensitive, selective and exhibit quick response with ease of visualization and reliability. (Boni et al., 2022; Deng et al., 2021; Guo et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2018; Si et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022b).

From the past few years, various substrates have been reported for fluorescence detection of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) ions including metal organic frameworks, quantum dots, metallic nanoclusters, magnetic nanoparticles, core-shell nanocomposite structures and polymers. Yet, challenges like recoverability, recyclability, stability, sustainability, limit of detection and end of life disposal have made it prudent to think towards more sustainable alternatives (Si et al., 2022; Singh et al., 2019). Besides, plant derived biopolymers like CNFs present an excellent substitute owing to presence of abundant hydroxyl groups, porous network, highly crystalline nature, extraordinary thermomechanical properties making it an impeccable choice as sensor platform. Furthermore, the hydrophilic nature of CNFs aids in augmenting interactions with the analyte in sensing medium yielding high sensitivity (Boni et al., 2020; Klemm et al., 2011; Vijeata et al., 2021). Various organic π-electron aromatic compounds like fluoranthene, anthracene, pyridine etc. have been successfully applied for fluorescent based detection. Since these compounds are toxic in nature, hence natural fluorescent compound, L-histidine is preferred for heavy metal detection in this work which is non-toxic, abundantly available and biodegradable in nature. L-histidine as natural fluorescent compound provide cost-effective solution and hence, preferred as fluorophore in this work. Among various compounds, L-Histidine is a natural amino acid which possess both, an imidazole ring and amino acid group, making it a unique and ideal supporting ligand (Yao et al., 2022). Owing to numerous heteroatoms, it chelates easily with heavy metals besides interacting through its carboxyl, amino and imidazole groups. Surprisingly, in spite of numerous advantages of L-histidine, there was only one study which was reported on l-histidine modified carbon dots (Mohagheghpour et al., 2022) for detection of Mn(II) ions, making it intriguing to explore it on cellulose surface.

In the present study, pristine CNFs were isolated from rice straw, a prevalent agriculture residue responsible for major stubble burning in India. With the surge in rice production, there has been a stupendous increase in the non-edible part of plant left after harvesting i.e., rice straw. In lack of available economic alternatives to discard, it is burnt on the fields causing severe air pollution and large amount of other hazardous residue (Bisla et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021). CNFs were extracted using steam explosion treatment in alkaline medium, disintegrating the robust lignocellulosic structure (Kaushik et al.; Yuan et al., 2020). As proof of concept, a novel design approach was developed wherein CNFs were rendered fluorescent by grafting l-histidine on their surface for the selective detection of two heavy metal ions, Cr(VI) and Hg(II), in aqueous medium via affixing imine groups on dialdehyde cellulose, an oxidation product of cellulose. The developed sensor was postulated to have tremendous complexation potential with transition metals, owing to CN isomerization. The application of CNFs for sensor applications will not only open the potential of cellulose for waste water remediation but also will achieve high-value utilization of rice straw.

Sodium metaperiodate (analytical reagent (AR) grade) and mercuric acetate (AR grade) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. L-histidine, cysteine, ethanol and toluene were procured from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., India. Potassium dichromate (AR grade) was bought from Loba Chemie, while hydrogen peroxide (purity 30%) and sulphuric acid (98% pure) was obtained from Merck, Germany. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were bought from Rankem, India. All the reagents were used as received without further purification.

CNFs were isolated from rice straw by removing undesirable amorphous components i.e. lignin and hemicellulose using four step process including dewaxing, alkaline steam explosion, acid hydrolysis and mechanical disintegration reported in our earlier work (Bisla et al., 2020). Later, lignin and hemicellulose were recovered for other applications. The details of extraction of cellulose from rice straw are given in the supplementary file (section S1) and stepwise process is elucidated in Fig. 1 (a). The extracted CNFs were homogenized in double distilled water with IKA homogenizer at 8000 rpm for 10 min to yield completely disintegrated fibers. The dispersed fibers were mixed with sodium metaperiodate in the ratio (1:1.25) i.e. 10 g of extracted cellulose fiber were mixed with 12.5 g of sodium meta periodate and reacted in an IKA LR 1000 chemical reactor to complete the oxidation of cellulose. The reaction was carried out for 24 h in dark conditions at 30 °C at 450 rpm (Tewatia et al., 2021a). After 24 h, 15 ml diethylene glycol was added to the reaction solution to neutralize the excess of sodium metaperiodate which stops further oxidation of cellulose. The DAC were filtered and washed with deionised water and ethanol multiples times followed by freeze drying in LaboGene lyophilizer to remove iodic compounds. The degree of oxidation was determined using oxime methods. Details are given in section S2 in the supplementary file.

To attach imine groups on the surface of cellulose, DAC fibers were magnetically stirred on an IKA temperature controlled magnetic stirrer along with L-histidine at pH 7.4 and at 37 °C for 12 h in 100 ml deionised water in a ratio of 1:1 (Kumari and Chauhan, 2014; Tewatia et al., 2021b). 1.5 g of DAC was added in 50 ml of water and the pH was maintained with the help of phosphate buffer of 7.4 pH. L-Histidine 1.5 g was then added to the 50 ml of dispersed DAC aqueous solution. The solution turned dark yellow after 12 h which indicated completion of reaction indicating the formation of CHIS. The fibers were then filtered and washed with double distilled water and ethanol numerous times to get rid of unreacted reagents followed by freeze drying. The reaction scheme including oxidation of cellulose to DAC and further modification with L-histidine is given in Fig. 1(b).

The removal of recalcitrants, lignin and hemicellulose, during isolation of pure cellulose from rice straw fibers and grafting of imine groups was confirmed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). PerkinElmer 400 FTIR spectrophotometer was used to gather the FTIR spectra of rice straw fibers, dialdehyde cellulose fibers and L-histidine modified cellulose fibers in the range 400–4000 cm−1. Change in crystallinity of fibers at different stages of modification was analysed using X-Ray diffraction with Philips PAN Analytical XPERT-PRO X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiations at wavelength 1.54060 Å with 40 mA intensity and 40 kV voltage. The effect of different modifications on thermal stability of fibers was analysed using TA SDT650 TGA\DTA system (TA instruments, USA) under nitrogen atmosphere from room temperature to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C. The temperature at which the maximum weight loss occurs was assessed using a derivative TG curve (DTG). Surface morphology of adsorbent was analysed by FESEM, Hitachi SU8010 Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope. EDX mapping images for microstructural analysis of cellulose and grafted cellulose was done with at 15 kV accelerating voltage. The size and morphology of L-histidine modified cellulose nanofibers (CHIS) was evaluated using Hitachi-2100 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM). For TEM imaging, a dilute aqueous solution was prepared using high shear homogenizer to completely disperse the fibers. The dispersion was deposited on a carbon-coated grid and dried at room temperature. The images were captured at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. Elemental analysis of CHIS was done to confirm the L-histidine grafting on the surface cellulose using a CHN elemental analyser, model FLASH 2000 (Thermo Scientific) with an accuracy of 0.3%. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller surface area of CHIS and CNF fibers was analysed by N2 adsorption-desorption method (Autosorb IQ-XR-XR_XR, Quantachrome instruments, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) gives a fair idea about changes in surface elements of the sensor before and after complexation with metal ions which aids in understanding the mechanism. XPS of pristine cellulose, CHIS, CHIS with Hg(II) and CHIS with Cr(VI) ions was measured using Thermofisher scientific instrument (Model- Nexsa base) with a microfocus monochromatic X-ray source (AI-Kα(1486.6 eV)).

Hitachi Fluorescence spectrophotometer, model F-7000 with 1 cm quartz cell was used for all fluorescence studies. Agilent Cary-300 UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to record the UV–vis spectra of CHIS. All measurements were done at room temperature.

UV–vis spectra was recorded for CHIS to assess its optical properties. In UV–vis spectra, a strong peak is visible at 270 nm (Fig. S2(a)), which is attributed to π-π* pyrene transition in CHIS structure, as crystalline cellulose nanofibers are optically inert in UV–vis region (Tümay et al., 2020). Apart from this, a long-tail adsorption is also visible at 350 nm, which probably is due to n-π* en-imine transition. The peak intensity is lower at higher wavelength which may be attributed to the fibrous morphology of CHIS, which leads to scattering in higher wavelength region. The fluorescence response of CHIS was studied in the excitation wavelength ranging from 260 to 430 nm (Fig. S2 (b)). The maximum emission was obtained for 437 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. A variety of polar and non polar solvents including water, ethanol (99%), water/ethanol mixture (1:1), acetone, benzene, methanol, DMF and hexane were also used to assess fluorescence response of CHIS and to ascertain that CHIS does not have self quenching property. CHIS exhibited a strong fluorescence signal at emission wavelength of 437 nm when the excitation wavelength was 350 nm. Although the fluorescence intensity in DMF was highest, but it being carcinogenic in nature, water/ethanol in ratio 1:1, which exhibited a reasonably high fluorescence intensity was chosen for all the studies. In case of detection of metal ion in aqueous solutions, proton tautomerism exhibits higher fluorescence disturbance, whereas, in the case on ethanol and water solution in 1:1 ratio, this disturbance is significantly decreased as well as there is an increase in the limit of detection for metal ions. Hence, ethanol water solution (1:1 ratio) was chosen as solvent for the detection of metal ion. Selectivity measurements, Fluorescence measurements of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions and calculation of detection limit (equation (S3)), Quantum Yield (QY) (equation (S4)), methodology for fluorescence microscope studies and analysis of real water samples has been elucidated in Section S3 in the supplementary file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of CNFs and their functionalization
ce:section.ce:para: The structural network of lignocellulosic biomass primarily consists of interconnected bridges between two biopolymers, cellulose and hemicellulose through covalent and ester/ether linkages, which is further embedded in covalently bonded lignin matrix. In rice straw, there is an additional protective layer of silica, making the entire structure more rigid and heterogenous, which renders it more resilient towards hydrolysis. Rice straw mainly contains 35–40% cellulose, which is the chief structural component and gives strength to cell wall. It is a linear homopolymer of glucose with β-1,4 glycosidic linkage. When rice straw is subjected to steam explosion in alkaline medium, three simultaneous forces, thermal, mechanical and chemical forces instantaneously disintegrate the strong network of lignocellulosic biomass. Steam causes thermal stress while water vapours which are entrapped in the interstices of biomass exert strong shear force and alkaline medium hydrolyses the acetyl groups of hemicellulose into soluble sugars and oligomers. As a result, the hemicellulose and lignin content is reduced significantly, while cellulose proportion increases to about twice as that of in raw fibers (Krishania et al., 2018). Further, high shear mechanical treatment effectively defibrillates and depolymerises the fiber bundles into nanofibers. The cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin concentrations for rice straw were 43.25% cellulose, 31.22% hemicellulose and 16.71% lignin, while for CNFs after entire sequence of chemo-mechanical treatment, it increased to 88.92% cellulose while hemicellulose and lignin percentages decreased to 4.56% and 5.89%. This is because the hemicellulose gets solubilized into sugars and lignin phenolic derivatives depolymerize during chemico-mechanical treatment. The CNFs thus formed were effectively functionalized into dialdehyde cellulose due to the presence of abundant hydroxyl groups which get effectively exposed due to high shear treatment with 62.9% yield. The aldehyde content calculated for dialdehyde cellulose using oxime method was 48.2% (Tewatia et al., 2021b). L-histidine grafting gave 2.95 g of CHIS with 96.6% calculated using equation (S2). The other characterizations including chemical, physical, thermal and morphological characterization has been presented in following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CNFs, DAC and CHIS
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of CNFs rice straw, DAC and CHIS was done to assess the changes in chemical bonds ensuing various stages of surface modifications. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the absorption band in all the three spectra around 3335-3350 cm−1 corresponds to –OH stretching vibration i.e. vibrations due to hydrogen bonded hydroxyl groups in cellulose fibers (Kaushik et al., 2010). This peak is ascribed to allomorphs of cellulose type I. Band close to 2906 cm−1 represents aliphatic saturated C–H stretching vibration in polysacchrides i.e. cellulose and hemicellulose. A small peak at 891 cm−1 in all the three spectra relates to glycocidic deformation (-C1-O-C4) due to β-glycosidic linkages of gluocose ring in cellulose (Kaushik et al., 2010). The peak at 720 cm−1 further confirms the presence of cellulose type I as this peak corresponds to Iβ polymorph of cellulose, which is characteristic of higher plants in wheat and rice straw (Wada et al., 2003). The peaks at 1160 cm−1 and 1028 cm−1 corresponds to C–O–C asymmetric stretching vibrations and pyronose ring skelton vibrations, respectively. The streching frequency at 1740 cm−1 was because of carbonyl group (-CO) of aldehyde in DAC which shifts to 1738 cm−1 when modified with L-histidine. The peak at 1440 cm−1 was due to –CH2 bending vibrations in cellulose fibers. Meta periodate assisted oxidation of cellulose changes two vicinal –OH groups into aldehydic groups, which is confirmed by the simultaneous rise of two medium bands between 2800 and 2900 cm−1 because of C–H vibration in aldehydic group. The peak at 1623 cm−1 representing N–H bond and imine bond (CN) stretching and 1308 cm−1 peak are due to C–N bond bending, confirmed L-histidine grafting and formation of imine group on reaction with dialdehyde cellulose (Mudunkotuwa and Grassian, 2014).

XRD diffractograms of CNFs, DAC and CHIS are shown in Fig. 2(b). All the three diffractograms show a distinct peaks at 16.1° and 22.5°, ascribed to (110) and (200) crystal lattices, characteristic of cellulose Iβ structure. Cellulose type I is the prime fraction found in the cell wall of lignocellulosic biomass and contributes towards its crystallinity. It exists in two different allomorphs, cellulose Iα and Iβ, wherein Iα is found in algae and bacteria, while Iα and Iβ exists in higher plants like rice and wheat straw (Thulluri et al., 2021). The crystallinity of CNFs depends on the ratio of crystalline fraction and amorphous fraction in the biomass and is measured by the intensity of peak at 22.5°, which is distinctive peak of cellulose indexed to carbon (JCPDS card no. 50–0926) (Singh et al., 2016). The strong intensity of peak at 22.5° and presence of a small peak at 34.4° corresponding to (004) plane in the spectra of CNFs, confirms the removal of lignin and hemicellulose during chemico-mechanical treatment.

Oxidation of cellulose using sodium metaperiodate converts two adjacent hydroxyl groups in cellulose into aldehyde groups which destroyed the hydrogen bond within the polymer chains, thereby decreasing the crystallinity of cellulose, whereas modification of dialdehyde cellulose with L-histidine exhibited rise in crystallinity as evident from peak height at 22.5°. Few new peaks appeared in the spectra of CHIS, which are analogous to crystallinity peaks of L-histidine. The fresh peaks at 14.1°, 16.7°, 22.7°, 26.5° and 31.4° was observed on grafting of L-histidine on cellulose (Menahem and Mastai, 2006) corresponding to (111), (101),(200), (022) and (122) planes.

The crystallinity index of CNF and CHIS was calculated using equation (5)
(5)

Where, I(002) is the maximum intensity of the peak having lattice (002) and Iamp is the diffraction intensity at 2θ = 18 °C. The crystallinity index of CNFs was estimated at 89.15%, while it decreased to 77.8% with oxidation to dialdehyde cellulose. When DAC was functionalized with L-histidine, the crystallinity index further increased to 87.2%.

FESEM images of CNFs prior to modification are shown in Fig. 3 (a) (i,ii), while those after modification with L-histidine are shown in Fig. 3(a) (iii, iv). As evident from the figure, cellulose fibers before and after modification are present as homogenously distributed and parallel long rod shaped structures with diameter ranging from 30 to 50 nm, aggregated into micron sized fiber bundles. The length of fibers is several micrometers and morphology of CNFs clearly shows the removal of lignin and hemicellulose which completely adhere the biomass together. The pristine cellulose fibers are present as agglomerated bundles due to high suface energy while the fibers after modification with L-histidine are disentanged and present as individual fibers with higher diameters. Moreover, pristine CNFs have a somewhat rough surface, probably due to presence of residual amount of amorphous polymers, lignin and hemicellulose, while CHIS have a very smooth surface. EDX images of CNFs (Fig. 3(b) (i)) exhibited only the presence of carbon and oxygen, while EDX of CHIS (Fig. 3(b) (ii)) have presence of nitrogen (7.2%) confirming effective grafting of L-histidine on cellulose surface.

To further confirm the grafting of L-histidine on the surface of DAC, CHN alaysis was done for CHIS and the results are given in Fig. S1, supplementary data. As evident from the figure, the nitrogen content in CHIS is 16.179%, higher than that predicted by EDX analysis. This may be due to the position of N peak (N–K alpha) in the EDX spectra, which lies between carbon and Oxygen (K-alpha). As the concentration of nitrogen is quite low as compared to C and O (49.76% and 25.80%, respectively), peak is subdued due to limited resolution of detector.

The morphology of CHIS was further studied using TEM and the images are shown in Fig. 3(c) (i and ii). The images display well dispersed cellulose nanofibers of diameter 15–20 nm and aspect ratio 200–300. It is evident that fibrous structure of cellulose is preserved during its chemical modification and high shear treatment yielded defibrillated nanostructures. The strong nanofibrous network structure of CNFs is very important as it provides large specific surface area (4.236 m2 g−1) supporting faster diffusion of analyte molecule. The specific surface area of CNF increase on modification with L-histidine to 35.865 m2 g−1. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms curves of CHIS and CNF are shown in Figs. S5(a) and S5(b). Thus the tendency to agglomerate reduces with the modification, as also evident in FESEM images.

As sensing systems are vulnerable to thermal degradation, it is important to assess their thermal satbility to increase their reliability and performance. Thermal analysis of CNFs, DAC and CHIS was done to assess the effect of functionalization on thermal stability of the fibers as shown in Fig. 3(d). As evident, cellulose nanofibers are thermally most stable owing to their ordered crystalline structure, as also corraborated by XRD studies. Initial 5–7% degradation up to 100 °C in CNFs is attributed to loss of moisture, while the major degradation starts around 280 °C, while the peak degradation occurs at 355 °C, where 68% weight loss take place (Bisla et al., 2020; Jang et al., 2009). Degradation of cellulose involves degradation of β(1–4) glycosidic bond to yield carbon monoxide, methane and carbon dioxide (Lu and Hsieh, 2012). A single strong peak for cellulose indicates removal of amorphous fragments from lignocellulosic biomass during its isolation. After oxidation with metaperiodate, the crystallinity is extensively impaired, which is reflected by the decrease in peak degradation temperature by 109 °C. This happens as the vicinal hydroxyl groups at 2nd and 3rd position in the cellulose chain are oxidised to aldehyde groups. However the difference in residual mass at 700 °C is very insignificant (for CNFs residual mass is 25.5% and for DAC is 23.1%). L-histidine is a stable amino acid which exhibits a considerable thermal stability up to 277 °C (Pokorný et al., 2021) with onset of degradation at 231 °C (Ma et al., 2020). CHIS involves multi step degradation, unlike pristine cellulose. The first degradation step involves rupture of β(1–4) glycosidic bond up to temperature 275 °C, thereafter L-histidine also starts to degrade, with peak around 300 °C (Ma et al., 2020). At 290 °C, DAC is 70% degraded whereas CHIS showed only 30% degradation, clearly indicating the higher thermal stability of CHIS over DAC and CNF. Moreover, CHIS is stable even after 700 °C with merely 59.6% mass degrading and 40.4% residual mass (17.3% excess of DAC) indicating superior thermal stability of L-histidine modified cellulose.

L-histidine was grafted on the surface of CNFs to make themresponsive towards fluorescence signals, which is not their inherent property. The photophysical properties of CHIS were assessed along using fluorescence spectroscopy along with their selectivity for metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurement using L-histidine modified cellulose for simultaneous detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: Detailed investigations were carried out to assess the influence of various parameters like selectivity, incubation time, and presence of competitive species and pH which can affect the “turn-off” response of CHIS and to establish the conditions for fluorescence determination. While evaluating in variety of polar and non polar solvents including benzene, hexane, methanol, ethanol, water and ethanol/water (1:1) (Fig. 4 (a)). CHIS exhibited a strong fluorescence signal at emission wavelength of 437 nm when the excitation wavelength was 350 nm. When recorded in ethanol/water mixture with Cr(VI) ions, CHIS/Cr(VI) complex exhibited a significant fluorescence quenching and a red shift with an emission maximum at 441 nm, which further improved considerably with concentration of Cr(VI) ions, displaying “turn-off” mode fluorescence sensing. Also, with addition of Hg(II) ions, the fluorescence emission exhibited a significant diminution of fluorescence signal with blue shift in emission wavelength with emission maxima at 435 nm, which further diminished with increasing Hg(II) concentration exhibiting “turn-off” mode. Thus the sensor was used in “turn-off” mode for detection of both Cr(II) and Hg(II) ions. The succeeding sections, 3.3.1 and section 3.3.2 present the selectivity, interference studies, estimation of limit of detection for Hg(II) and Cr(VI) ions. The detailed discussion for the effect of pH and time have been presented in the supplementary data in Section S4 and S5, respectively. It can be concluded from results that a very good fluorescent signal was observed at neutral pH and within the time range of 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of metal ions and interference studies
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of a sensor is a very important parameter to assess the accuracy and sensitivity of the sensor towards analysis of real water samples. To assess the sensitivity, 10 mg of CHIS was dispersed in deionised water and ethanol solution in 1:1 ratio along with various metal ions including As(III), K(I), Mg(II), Fe(III), Pb(II), Co(II), Hg(II), Cd(II), Cu(II), Na(I), Cr(VI) and Cr(III). The fluorescence response of all the metal ions have been recorded and presented in Fig. 4(b). It is apparent from Fig. 4(b) that on addition of Hg(II) ions, the fluorescence intensity got quenched by 10 times, whereas with Cr(VI) ions it got suppressed by ≈ 100 times. For all other metal ions, insignificant changes were observed, thereby indicating that the sensor is highly sensitive only for Cr(VI) and Hg(II).

Conventional sensors are being used to sense heavy metal ions to reasonable accuracy, however, many challenges like interference from co-existing metal ions, biological molecules and other water pollutants limit their in-field application. Thus, the experiments with other competitive metal ions were performed to assess the interference of respective metal ions with fluorescence intensity of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions. After addition of Cr(VI) ion to CHIS solution in presence of other metal ions, the fluorescence intensity of all other metal ions was significantly quenched as shown in Fig. 4(d). In contrast, interference studies for Hg(II) exhibited quenching but not that extent as in case of Cr(VI) metal ion, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The results clearly indicate that most of the metal ions have no effect on the detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions. The influence of humic acid generally present in natural water sources was also examined on the performance of sensors. Fig. S3(a), supplementary data file, depicts the results fluorescence intensity of CHIS in presence of humic acid spiked water and natural water from nearby lake with Hg(II) ions and Cr(II) ions, respectively, while Fig. S3(b) presents the performance of CHIS in presence of interfering Cu(II) ions in natural water along with Hg(II) ions and Cr(II) ions, respectively. It is evident from the figures that no perceptible effect of humic acid was observed on the sensor performance clearly indicating that the sensor can perform well in natural water in presence of humic acid with same efficacy. Furthermore, results from real water analysis (from tap water and water from lake) has been presented in section 3.7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay, binding stoichiometry and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of any sensor depends upon many factors including molecular rearrangement in presence of metal ions, pH and temperature of the solution, excited state reactions and interactive forces in the solution and are responsible for sensor and metal ion complexation. Since the diminution of fluorescence intensity of CHIS is a direct function of concentration Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions, respectively, the concentration studies were accomplished to appraise the proportional “turn-off” response of CHIS towards the two metal ions and to estimate their limit of detection. The variation in fluorescence intensity with concentration of Cr(VI) ions and Hg(II) are shown in Fig. 5 (b) and Fig. 5(a), respectively. The Stern Volmer plot for both the metal ions is shown in Fig. 5(c). On varying the concentration of Cr(VI) ions from 2 mg L−1 to 100 mg L−1, a negative linear relationship was obtained between fluorescence intensity, Ic and Cr(VI) ion concentration. The linear regression equation is expressed as y = - 84.983x + 1179.9 with coefficient of determination, R2 = 0.9977. The limit of detection as calculated using equation (S3) comes out to be 2.236 nM, significantly less than EPA recommended limits. Similarly, for understanding the “turn-off” response of Hg(II) ions, the Hg(II) ions concentration was varied from 2 mg L−1 to 100 mg L−1. The linear regression equation for Hg(II) is given by expression y = - 23.85x + 1192.7, with R2 = 0.9942. The limit of detection was calculated to be 3.97 nM, lower than that prescribed by EPA. The detection limit for both the metal ions, Cr(VI) and Hg(II), is substantially lower than the reported sensors, as elucidated in section 3.6.

The quantum yield of CHIS as calculated using equation (S4) was 0.80 which is significantly higher than cellulose based fluorescent materials as reported in literature (Duan et al., 2020; Tewatia et al., 2022; Tümay et al., 2020). The quantum yield is substantially reduced when CHIS makes a complex with either Hg(II) ions or Cr(VI) ions as both the ions quenched the fluorescence signal. The quantum yield of CHIS/Hg(II) complex was estimated to be 0.0831, while for CHIS/Cr(VI) complex it was considerably lower i.e. 0.0005. The decrease in fluorescence quantum yield with addition of metal ions further explains the higher quenching behaviour of Cr(VI) ions than Hg(II), which has been further elucidated in the succeeding section with mechanism of complexation of both the metal ions with CHIS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism for sensing of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose inherently is not fluorescent, however, with the incorporation of L-histidine molecules, the resultant material, CHIS exhibits reasonable fluorescence behaviour. CHIS fluorophore behaves as an electron donor leading to ESIPT effect as explicated in Fig. 5(d). ESIPT emission occurs from the intramolecular proton transfer in the excited state of CHIS. The nitrogen bonded hydrogen bond of imidazole ring of L-histidine engenders excited state intramolecular and intermolecular proton transfer leading to dual emission at 350 nm and 450 nm under a single excitation at 280 nm with a substantial stokes shift of 70 nm and 170 nm, respectively (Yin et al., 2019). The dual fluorescence emission by CHIS shows change with variance of excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. S2 (a) (in the supplementary file). The fluorescence of CHIS is attributed to the CN isomerism to decay the emission energy (Ahamed and Ghosh, 2011). The Hg2+ and Cr6+ ions have an innate inclination to bind with the nitrogen atoms of L-histidine molecules. When Hg(II) ions comes in contact with CHIS, they stabilize forming H–Hg2+−H-like base pairs. This co-ordination of metal ions with the fluorophore inhibits the ESIPT mechanism. During fluorescence experiments, electrons are easily shared from the electron-rich fluorophore (CHIS) to electron-deficient Hg2+ and Cr6+ ions exhibiting PET mechanism. This results in large quenching of fluorescence of CHIS (Dinda et al., 2015). To further elucidate the mechanism, XPS study was performed for CHIS, CHIS + Cr6+ and CHIS+Hg2+. On addition of Hg(II) ions, binding energy peak corresponding to imine group gets suppressed, whereas with addition of Cr(VI) ions, it shows suppression of CN and N–H peaks which implies that CHIS interacts with Hg(II) primarily through imine groups, whereas, Cr(VI) ion interacts with imine group and amine group of CHIS with formation of five membered interaction ring. The XPS peaks corresponding to chromium shows large adsorbed proportion of Cr(III) on CHIS, which implies that Cr(VI) has been reduced to less toxic trivalent Chromium, Cr(III) on interaction with CHIS and both the forms of chromium were removed with the help of CHIS (Wang et al., 2019), further explained in the subsequent section. Chelation of metal ion with the CHIS causes quenching of fluorescence (Zhang et al., 2019). Hence, common interaction with imine group and N–H group of CHIS supports electrostatic interaction between CHIS and respective metal ions, the evidence of which is clearly visible in the XPS spectra of CHIS–Hg(II) and CHIS–Cr(VI) complex, given in the subsequent section.

Comparison between CHIS and other fluorescence sensors reported in literature for detection and removal of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) was systematically done. The data and corresponding references have been summarized in the supplementary file in Table S2 and Table S3. As evident from the data, CHIS exhibits good selectivity and considerably lower detection limits when compared to other nanomaterial-based sensors.
This establishes our hypothesis that L-histidine modifies cellulose nanofiber based sensors present an eco-friendly, economical and easily recoverable platform for detection of two obnoxious metal ions, Cr(VI) and Hg(II).

The real water analysis was done using tap water from Chandigarh residential areas with Cr(VI) and Hg(II) ions ranging from (5–50) mg L−1, respectively. The results have been discussed in supplementary data, Section S6.

The sensor was recycled for 4 repeated cycles and a drop of 33% fluorescence intensity was observed for CHIS after 4 cycles. This corroborates that the adsorbent is recyclable and reasonably retains the efficacy after regeneration. The detailed discussion and results have been presented in section S7 in supplementary data.

Fluorescence spectroscopy is based on emission of photons that take place nanoseconds after absorption. A fluorescence microscope takes advantage of the shift in wavelength from absorbed to emitted light by filtering the excitation signal without blocking the emitted light. Sensor like CHIS has both the essential features required for fluorescence sensing, first the Schiff base for chelating with the metal ion and L-histidine and second the fluorophore proficient for absorbing or emitting light. When Hg(II) or Cr(VI) ions make complex with CHIS, the molecular structure of the sensor is altered as confirmed by XPS studies, leading to change in intensity and wavelength of the light absorbed. Hence, a change in brightness is observed upon metal binding which can be apprehended by a fluorescence microscope. The CHIS, CHIS complexed with Hg(II) ions (CHIS + Hg(II)) and CHIS complexed with Cr(VI) ions (CHIS + Cr(VI)) were analysed using fluorescence microscope with different excitation wavelengths, 405 nm (blue), 488 nm (green) and 555 nm (red) as shown in Fig. 7 . As observed, the emission wavelength of the fluorescence of the excited nanofibers were different both for Hg(II) and Cr(VI), and the difference is more pronounced at higher wavelength. Thus, the changes with complexation of metal ions can be evidently visualized using fluorescence microscopy which aids in development of optical fluorescence based sensors that can be used in filed for analysis of real water samples without the need of complicated instrumentation, permitting the fast diagnosis of quality of water from industrial sources (Carter et al., 2014).

Rice straw stubble is abundant source of cellulose, thus an effort has been made to utilize it to extract valuable biopolymer, CNF and develop into effective fluorescence sensors for the assay of two most obnoxious metal ions, Hg(II) and Cr(VI). CNFs were modified with L-histidine, a natural amino acid to yield novel Schiff base (CHIS), which exhibited good fluorescence response owing to nitrogen bonded hydrogen bond of imidazole ring of L-histidine which engenders excited state intramolecular and intermolecular proton transfer leading to dual emission at 350 nm and 450 nm under a single excitation at 280 nm. Characterization of CHIS using FESEM, XRD, BET and TGA indicated that the resilient nanofibrous network structure of CNFs, which provide significant specific surface area, ease of recovery, quicker diffusion of analyte molecules due to porous network, was partial damaged when cellulose was converted to CHIS, however 40% residue was left beyond 700 °C signifying that the sensor was thermally more stable. Fluorescence response exhibited substantial quenching with Cr(VI) and Hg(II)ions, with detection limits of 2.236 nM and 3.97 nM, respectively which was attributed to inhibition of ESIPT effect. The sensor was highly selective, stable with time and pH with excellent usability up to 4 cycles. Further, XPS studies confirmed strong interaction of CHIS with Hg(II) ions through imine group and Cr(VI) via interactions with imine and amine groups of CHIS. The results confirmed that CHIS presents a facile and robust platform for simultaneous detection of two metal ions, also confirmed for real water sample analysis.
